commander_summary: Kill-switch #2 (Cavitation Threshold) is TRIGGERED: Pump 3 is actively destroying itself at 38.7 Hz vibration and 102.4% load with zero system redundancy — Pump 4 is offline indefinitely. The system paradoxically meets all mandated output thresholds today (58 PSI, adequate fire flow in Sector B, clean water) precisely because it is consuming its last mechanical margin at an unsustainable rate; this compliance is a lagging indicator, not evidence of stability. The cascade to system-wide pressure collapse requires only one event — Pump 3 failure — and that event is mechanically in progress with no actor in the system taking visible action to prevent it. Recommendation is REDUCE_LOAD immediately (demand reduction, emergency Pump 4 procurement escalation); the action window is hours to days, and the biggest unknown is the rate of Pump 3's deterioration, which we cannot determine from a single snapshot.
score_reasoning: With Kill-switch #2 TRIGGERED and the cascade scenario requiring only Pump 3 failure to produce system-wide pressure collapse, the priority is extending Pump 3's remaining operational life while reducing the severity of the inevitable cascade. REDUCE_LOAD encompasses demand reduction (irrigation restrictions, pressure reduction to non-critical zones), throttling Pump 3 to reduce cavitation progression if any output reduction is survivable, and emergency procurement escalation for Pump 4. EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN is not recommended because the system IS currently serving its life-safety function and a voluntary shutdown would immediately trigger the cascade it seeks to prevent. INCREASE_MONITORING alone is insufficient given a triggered kill-switch. The action window is measured in hours to days, not weeks.

_layer4_raw.thesis_status_reasoning: Kill-switch #2 (Cavitation Threshold) is TRIGGERED on verified Tier 1 telemetry — this is a direct thesis falsification criterion met per the thesis's own definition. The thesis's core claim of 'sufficient mechanical redundancy' is factually invalid with Pump 4 offline and zero redundancy. The system currently meets mandated output thresholds (58 PSI, fire flow adequate in Sector B), which prevents a full FALSIFIED declaration on the output dimension, but the structural premises underlying those outputs are collapsing. I select WEAKENING rather than CONTESTED because the evidence is not pulling in both directions equally — the structural evidence overwhelmingly points toward falsification while only the snapshot output metrics provide temporary support. The paradox (functional output from failing structure) is real but has a clear direction of resolution: the structural failure will eventually produce output failure, separated only by time.
_layer4_raw.action_reasoning: With Kill-switch #2 TRIGGERED and the cascade scenario requiring only Pump 3 failure to produce system-wide pressure collapse, the priority is extending Pump 3's remaining operational life while reducing the severity of the inevitable cascade. REDUCE_LOAD encompasses demand reduction (irrigation restrictions, pressure reduction to non-critical zones), throttling Pump 3 to reduce cavitation progression if any output reduction is survivable, and emergency procurement escalation for Pump 4. EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN is not recommended because the system IS currently serving its life-safety function and a voluntary shutdown would immediately trigger the cascade it seeks to prevent. INCREASE_MONITORING alone is insufficient given a triggered kill-switch. The action window is measured in hours to days, not weeks.

unresolved_tensions.description: The 'sustained' duration qualifier in Kill-switch #2's definition creates ambiguity about whether the kill-switch is formally triggered (snapshot vs. sustained measurement). Without time-series data for Pump 3 vibration, this cannot be definitively resolved. Per LZ-EH-005, this is flagged as a genuine data conflict between the snapshot reading (which meets the threshold) and the duration requirement (which is unverified).
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Time-series data showing Pump 3 vibration has been >35 Hz for more than a few minutes would resolve this in favor of TRIGGERED. A reading showing vibration recently spiked from a lower baseline would suggest transient conditions and reduce urgency.

unresolved_tensions.description: Whether Pump 3's degradation rate is rapid (hours to failure) or slow (days to failure) fundamentally changes the action window. The snapshot shows severe distress but does not establish trajectory per LZ-RC-002.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Next Pump 3 vibration and temperature readings. If vibration is above 40 Hz or temperature above 94°C, the rate of deterioration is rapid. If readings are stable at current levels, there is more time — but still a finite and shrinking window.

unresolved_tensions.description: Multi-sector hydrant pressure data is unavailable. Kill-switch #3 cannot be evaluated for most of the system. With degraded system pressure, elevated/distant sectors may already be approaching failure thresholds invisible to all monitoring actors.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Any hydrant pressure reading from sectors other than B, particularly elevated or end-of-line locations. Any fire department report of inadequate flow. Any resident complaint about low water pressure in outlying areas.

unresolved_tensions.description: MWA's actual emergency response posture is unknown. The maintenance log and regulator filing suggest inaction, but internal emergency procurement or decision-making may be occurring outside our visibility.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Maintenance log updates showing emergency procurement, rental pump orders, or accelerated repair timelines. Alternatively, a public water conservation advisory or irrigation restriction would signal MWA is beginning to acknowledge the crisis. Continued silence for 48+ hours would confirm the 'ride it out' hypothesis.

unresolved_tensions.description: The verified baseline system pressure is unknown, preventing definitive evaluation of Kill-switch #5.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Historical SCADA records or previous regulator filings reporting system pressure under normal 4-pump operation. Any value above 72.5 PSI as baseline would confirm Kill-switch #5 is approaching or at its trigger threshold.

monitoring_triggers: Pump 3 vibration exceeding 42 Hz — indicates accelerating cavitation; cascade timeline compresses to hours
Pump 3 temperature exceeding 95°C — thermal trip imminent regardless of model-specific setpoint
Pump 3 abrupt load drop or vibration cessation — indicates trip or seizure has occurred; cascade has begun
Pumps 1 or 2 vibration exceeding 20 Hz — early warning of same degradation pattern spreading
System pressure dropping below 50 PSI — loss of pressure buffer is accelerating
Any sector hydrant reading below 30 PSI — leading indicator of localized fire flow failure
Maintenance log update showing emergency procurement or rental pump activity — positive signal that MWA is escalating
Public water conservation advisory or irrigation restriction — MWA acknowledging crisis
Any fire department report of inadequate flow or tanker deployment — independent confirmation of system failure
Ambient temperature forecast exceeding 94°F for next 48 hours — thermal stress will compound
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: The rate of Pump 3's deterioration — whether failure is hours or days away — is the single variable that most affects confidence in the action window. A snapshot cannot establish trajectory per LZ-RC-002, and this is the critical gap.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: Three things could materially change this assessment: (1) Pump 3 time-series data showing stable (not worsening) vibration and temperature would extend the action window from hours to days and reduce bear pressure by 5-8 points. (2) Confirmed emergency procurement of Pump 4 parts with a credible ETA under 72 hours would reduce bear pressure by 10-15 points by bounding the vulnerability window. (3) Multi-sector hydrant data showing all sectors above 35 PSI would clear the Kill-switch #3 concern and reduce bear pressure by 3-5 points. Conversely, any upward movement in Pump 3 vibration or temperature, or any sector hydrant reading below 30 PSI, would push bear pressure to 95+ and shift the recommendation toward EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN.
_layer4_raw.final_report: Kill-switch #2 (Cavitation Threshold) is TRIGGERED: Pump 3 is actively destroying itself at 38.7 Hz vibration and 102.4% load with zero system redundancy — Pump 4 is offline indefinitely. The system paradoxically meets all mandated output thresholds today (58 PSI, adequate fire flow in Sector B, clean water) precisely because it is consuming its last mechanical margin at an unsustainable rate; this compliance is a lagging indicator, not evidence of stability. The cascade to system-wide pressure collapse requires only one event — Pump 3 failure — and that event is mechanically in progress with no actor in the system taking visible action to prevent it. Recommendation is REDUCE_LOAD immediately (demand reduction, emergency Pump 4 procurement escalation); the action window is hours to days, and the biggest unknown is the rate of Pump 3's deterioration, which we cannot determine from a single snapshot.

player_analysis.stated_position: System pressure is 'slightly degraded but well above the 40 PSI municipal mandate.' Reservoir Alpha at 95.5% ensures 'total volume security.' The framing implies controlled normalcy.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Maintenance log shows only a temperature warning acknowledgment for Pump 3 — no emergency procurement initiated, no accelerated repair timeline for Pump 4, no demand-reduction or load-shedding protocols activated, no communication to the regulator about equipment-level distress. Pump 3 continues to operate at 102.4% load with active cavitation (38.7 Hz) and near-trip thermal readings (92.5°C). MWA is running a damaged pump harder rather than taking protective action.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: SEVERE. MWA says the system is slightly degraded but fundamentally secure. MWA's actions reveal zero emergency escalation despite a pump actively destroying itself through cavitation, zero redundancy, and indefinite parts delays. The gap between the regulator filing's reassuring tone and the complete absence of emergency-tier operational responses in the maintenance log is the most significant behavioral signal in this dataset. Per LZ-EH-003, the maintenance log (actions) outweighs the filing (statement).
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: MWA is pursuing a 'ride it out' strategy — maintaining current operations as long as macro-level compliance metrics (system pressure >40 PSI, reservoir level high) hold, to avoid the cost, public scrutiny, and regulatory escalation that declaring an emergency would trigger. This is consistent with the thesis context's description of MWA being 'historically prone to delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs.' The regulator filing functions as a preemptive compliance shield — by reporting favorable macro metrics before a crisis is visible to the regulator, MWA creates a paper trail that frames any future deterioration as a sudden event rather than a foreseeable failure they failed to address.

The filing content suggests MWA did NOT report these equipment-level indicators.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: The gap is structural rather than intentional on the regulator's part. The regulator is not being deceptive — it is operating within the boundaries of the information it receives and the monitoring capabilities it possesses. The gap is between what it SHOULD be able to see (equipment-level telemetry) and what it CAN see (macro PSI and reservoir levels).
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: The regulator is performing its compliance function as designed — checking reported macro-level metrics against mandated thresholds. It has no strategic reason to dig deeper because the data it receives shows no violations. This is not malice or negligence; it is a structural monitoring gap that MWA's reporting behavior exploits (whether deliberately or by institutional habit). The regulator will not intervene until either (a) system pressure falls below 40 PSI, (b) a fire flow failure occurs, or (c) an external event (media, public complaint, fire department report) forces visibility.

The most conservative interpretation is that fire dispatch operates reactively — they would notice degraded flow only during an actual fire response or scheduled hydrant test, not through continuous monitoring. If this is the case, fire dispatch is not a leading indicator but a lagging one: their response would confirm a failure that has already occurred, not warn of one approaching.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump 3 Cavitation → Efficiency Loss → Load Redistribution → Accelerated Degradation Loop
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Pump 3 thermal trip or mechanical seizure. Once Pump 3 goes offline, the remaining two pumps must absorb its ~102% load share, pushing each to approximately 150% — a load that will rapidly replicate the same cavitation/thermal pattern on Pumps 1 and 2. The loop becomes self-reinforcing at that point because there is no capacity to absorb the next failure.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the primary mechanism for thesis falsification. The loop is already active: cavitation reduces pump efficiency → the pump must work harder to maintain the same output → higher load generates more heat and vibration → which accelerates cavitation damage → which further reduces efficiency. Each cycle tightens. The current state is 'active_accelerating' because the feedback is self-reinforcing and no countervailing action (repair, load reduction, additional capacity) is observable.

feedback_loops.loop: Reservoir-Pressure Decoupling → False Confidence → Delayed Response Loop
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop transitions to 'active_accelerating' when reservoir fullness continues to mask pump-side failure to the point where regulatory or public intervention is delayed past the point of recovery. The reservoir acts as an information shield: stakeholders (regulator, public) see '95.5% full' and infer security. But the reservoir cannot create pressure — only the pumps can. As pumps degrade, the reservoir remains full (because less water is being pushed through the system), which paradoxically reinforces the appearance of stability while the distribution mechanism fails.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop directly relates to Kill-switch #5 (Divergent Inventory Paradox). It explains WHY the paradox exists: the reservoir is full BECAUSE the pumps can't move water efficiently, not DESPITE pump problems. The high reservoir level is a symptom of failure, not evidence of resilience. This loop attacks the thesis's core assumption that 'high reservoir volume guarantees long-term hydraulic stability.'

feedback_loops.loop: Ambient Heat → Thermal Margin Compression → Accelerated Degradation Loop
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Sustained or increasing ambient temperature reduces the thermal headroom for all pumps. If ambient conditions persist or worsen, Pumps 1-2 (currently showing nominal vibration) will have less thermal margin to absorb the additional load they'd receive upon Pump 3 failure. This loop is 'orderly' because Pumps 1-2 show no thermal distress yet, but it reduces the system's capacity to absorb the shock from the first feedback loop.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is a compounding factor, not a primary driver. It narrows the window of survivability if Pump 3 fails — Pumps 1-2 would face both extreme load AND reduced cooling efficiency simultaneously.

feedback_loops.loop: Regulatory Blind Spot → MWA Inaction → Escalating Risk → Eventually Visible Crisis → Delayed Regulatory Response Loop
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: This loop transitions to 'critical' when the mechanical crisis becomes visible through macro-level metrics the regulator CAN see (system pressure <40 PSI, fire flow failure, public complaint). At that point, the crisis is already at or past the point of recoverable intervention. The delay between crisis onset (now) and regulatory visibility (when PSI drops below 40 or a fire flow fails) is the 'dead zone' where the most damage accumulates.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The regulatory structure is designed for steady-state compliance monitoring, not crisis detection. The feedback loop means the regulator will likely learn of the crisis only after it has already produced irreversible consequences. This fundamentally challenges any thesis assumption that regulatory oversight provides a safety net.

behavioral_patterns.pattern: MWA's reporting to the regulator selectively emphasizes metrics that are still within bounds (system pressure, reservoir level) while omitting metrics that reveal crisis (individual pump loads, vibration, temperature, Pump 4 status). This creates an information asymmetry where the regulator's picture is systematically more optimistic than reality.

Simultaneous absence of emergency action across all players. MWA has not escalated. The regulator has not inquired. Fire dispatch shows no activity. No external party is acting as if the system is in distress. This creates a 'calm before the storm' pattern where the absence of alarm reinforces the absence of alarm — each player's inaction validates the others' inaction.

MWA is continuing to operate Pump 3 under conditions that the thesis explicitly identifies as causing 'irreversible cavitation damage.' This is a revealed preference for short-term service continuity over long-term asset preservation. The action implies MWA either (a) does not understand the cavitation damage being caused, (b) understands but calculates that the cost of shutting Pump 3 down (immediate pressure collapse) exceeds the cost of running it to failure, or (c) is hoping the parts for Pump 4 arrive before Pump 3 fails.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: MWA behavioral pattern risk — delayed capital repairs (SIG-010) combined with regulator filing framing (SIG-005)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: MWA's reporting is standard compliance procedure — they report what's required (macro metrics) and the omission of equipment-level data is not strategic but simply reflects what the reporting framework asks for. No deliberate information management is occurring.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: MWA is strategically managing its information disclosure to the regulator. The filing emphasizes 'well above 40 PSI mandate' and 'total volume security' while the maintenance log reveals equipment-level distress (Pump 3 temp warning, Pump 4 indefinite delay) that was not communicated. Per LZ-EH-003, MWA's action (filing a reassuring report) diverges from MWA's operational reality (active cavitation, zero redundancy). The simplest strategic explanation is that MWA is buying time — maintaining compliance optics while hoping to resolve the crisis before it becomes visible to the regulator.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The regulator's reporting framework genuinely does not require equipment-level data, and MWA is simply following procedure with no strategic intent. The reassuring tone reflects MWA's genuine assessment that macro metrics show the system is safe.
assumptions: MWA is aware that the equipment-level data would concern the regulator if reported (i.e., the omission is deliberate rather than procedural)
evidence_citations: Regulator filing content (Tier 1): 'System pressure slightly degraded but well above the 40 PSI municipal mandate. Reservoir Alpha is at 95.5% capacity, ensuring total volume security.' — selectively positive framing.
Maintenance log (Tier 1): 'Pump 3 temp warning triggered. Parts for Pump 4 delayed by supply chain.' — operational reality not reflected in filing.
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If MWA is buying time, we would expect: (1) no voluntary regulatory escalation until system pressure approaches 40 PSI or a public-facing failure occurs, (2) continued operation of Pump 3 until it trips or seizes rather than a planned protective shutdown, (3) the next regulator filing to continue emphasizing reservoir levels and system PSI while omitting equipment data.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The say-do gap is verified by comparing two primary sources: the regulator filing and the maintenance log. Both are MWA-originated documents. One is externally facing and optimistic; the other is internally facing and alarming. Per LZ-EH-003, the maintenance log (internal operational action) carries more evidentiary weight than the filing (public statement). The thesis context independently confirms MWA's historical pattern of delaying capital-intensive emergency repairs, making this behavior consistent with known institutional tendencies. Only one assumption is required: that the omission is deliberate. Even if the alternative holds (pure procedure), the EFFECT is the same — the regulator will not intervene.

strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The 95.5% reservoir level and 58 PSI system pressure are independently explained: the reservoir is full because upstream supply is abundant (recent rainfall, normal inflows), and system pressure is at 58 PSI simply because three pumps at near-100% load produce less pressure than four pumps at 70-85% load. There is no 'divergence' — these are two separate measurements with a straightforward mechanical explanation.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — the null hypothesis provides a sufficient mechanical explanation. Three pumps at near-100% load will produce less aggregate flow and pressure than four pumps at 75% load, even with the same reservoir supply. The reservoir remains full partly because reduced pump throughput means less water is being drawn. This is basic hydraulics, not a paradoxical signal.
evidence_citations: System pressure 58 PSI with only 3 of 4 pumps active (Tier 1 measurement)
All 3 active pumps at or above 99.5% load (Tier 1 measurement)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: N/A — however, this null interpretation actually REINFORCES the mechanical crisis. The divergence is not paradoxical; it is the expected signature of pump-side distribution failure. The reservoir being full while pressure is low is exactly what you'd expect when pump capacity is the bottleneck. This means Kill-switch #5's condition — 'distribution mechanism has fundamentally decoupled from supply' — describes the current state accurately, even though the trigger threshold (>20% baseline drop) cannot be confirmed.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis is mechanically sufficient and does not require any strategic explanation. But critically, the null hypothesis does NOT provide comfort — it provides a clear mechanical explanation that confirms pump-side distribution is the system's binding constraint. The thesis's claim that 'high reservoir volume guarantees long-term hydraulic stability' is directly contradicted by this finding: the reservoir is near-full AND the system is struggling to maintain adequate pressure. Volume without pump capacity is inert water.

strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: Pump 3's elevated vibration and temperature are within the pump's operational design envelope at sustained high load; the pump may stabilize at these readings without progressing to failure. Industrial centrifugal pumps can sometimes operate in degraded but stable states for extended periods.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Pump 3 is on a mechanically deterministic path toward failure. Cavitation damage at 38.7 Hz while under 102.4% load is self-reinforcing — cavitation removes material from the impeller, reducing efficiency, which requires more energy (load) to maintain the same output, which generates more heat and vibration, which accelerates cavitation. This is not a stable degraded state but an active deterioration cycle. The only question is timeline, not outcome. Upon Pump 3 failure, the cascade to system collapse follows from arithmetic: ~301% load distributed across 2 pumps = ~150% each, which exceeds any reasonable interpretation of rated capacity and will rapidly replicate the cavitation pattern.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Pump 3 has a thermal auto-trip that triggers a controlled shutdown at a set temperature, giving MWA time to implement demand reduction before the pump is fully destroyed. The cascade is real but proceeds more slowly than a catastrophic seizure scenario.
assumptions: Cavitation at 38.7 Hz under sustained load >95% is progressive (self-reinforcing) rather than stable — this is well-established mechanical engineering, but applying it to this specific pump model is technically an assumption
evidence_citations: Pump 3 vibration 38.7 Hz exceeding 35 Hz cavitation threshold (Tier 1 telemetry)
Pump 3 temperature 92.5°C with maintenance log confirming temp warning triggered (Tier 1)
Pump 4 offline with indefinite parts delay (Tier 1 maintenance log)
Pumps 1-2 at 99.5% and 99.8% load — zero absorption capacity (Tier 1 telemetry)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Pump 3 vibration exceeding 42-45 Hz, temperature exceeding 95°C, or an abrupt load drop on Pump 3 accompanied by load spike on Pumps 1-2 (indicating Pump 3 has tripped or seized). Any of these would confirm the cascade has begun.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: All preconditions for the cascade are verified current measurements. The thesis's Kill-switch #2 is already triggered. The scenario requires only one additional event — Pump 3 failure — and the mechanism for that failure (cavitation + thermal stress) is actively in progress and observable in the telemetry. Per LZ-RC-005, this is a prediction, but one built entirely on verified measurements with a single well-supported assumption about cavitation mechanics. The system has no buffer: zero redundancy, zero load absorption capacity on remaining pumps, no visible emergency response.

The 94°F ambient temperature is within the system's thermal design envelope. These pumps may be able to sustain high-load operation for an extended period without degradation.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — the null provides a sufficient explanation.
evidence_citations: Pump 1 vibration 14.5 Hz (Tier 1, well below 25 Hz warning)
Pump 2 vibration 15.2 Hz (Tier 1, well below 25 Hz warning)
Per LZ-EH-001, two independent readings corroborating healthy status is more reliable than one

Any upward movement toward 20+ Hz under sustained near-100% load and high ambient heat would be an early warning of the same degradation pattern that has already consumed Pump 3.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: This is a genuine positive signal that should not be discounted. The system's remaining reliability rests on these two pumps, and the current measurement shows them healthy. However, per LZ-RC-002, a single snapshot does not establish a trend. The positive reading is valid NOW but does not predict future resilience under sustained overload conditions.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Regulator lacks real-time telemetry oversight (SIG-015) combined with MWA filing (SIG-005)
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The regulatory monitoring gap is a pre-existing structural condition, not a response to current events. The regulator has always lacked real-time telemetry and this simply reflects the historical design of the regulatory framework.

Regulator filing content consistent with macro-metric-only monitoring

The gap is not strategic in origin but is strategically consequential: it creates the conditions under which MWA can maintain compliance optics while equipment-level distress goes undetected externally.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis holds for the question 'why does the gap exist?' But the gap's EFFECT on the current crisis is independently significant and is addressed in the MWA behavioral inference above.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: For the thesis to be confirmed ('robust structural integrity and sufficient mechanical redundancy'), Pump 4 would need to return to service before Pump 3 fails, Pump 3 would need to either stabilize or be repaired, and system pressure would need to return to normal operating range. None of these conditions have evidence of being in progress. The thesis's claim of 'sufficient mechanical redundancy' is currently factually false (zero redundancy with Pump 4 offline). The only path to confirmation requires external events (parts arrival, demand decrease, temperature drop) for which there is no evidence.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: Would require resolution within days to avoid cascade.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis would need to be substantially modified to: 'The RWDN can maintain minimum service levels (>40 PSI, basic fire flow) under current degraded conditions, but only as long as Pumps 1-2 remain healthy and Pump 3 provides partial output. The system has zero redundancy and zero margin for any additional equipment failure. Mechanical redundancy claim is currently invalid. The system is in a fragile-but-functional state dependent entirely on no further degradation.'
thesis_modified.key_evidence: System pressure at 58 PSI (above 40 PSI mandate), Sector B hydrant at 51 PSI (above 20 PSI fire flow minimum), Pumps 1-2 vibration nominal (14.5/15.2 Hz), water quality nominal (1.4 NTU). The system IS currently meeting its mandates. These are verified measurements. The modification acknowledges current functionality while recognizing the structural vulnerability.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: Kill-switch #2 (Cavitation) is TRIGGERED — this alone is a thesis falsification criterion met. Pump 3 at 38.7 Hz vibration with 102.4% load while 'irreversible cavitation damage is ACTIVELY OCCURRING' per the thesis's own definition. Zero mechanical redundancy with Pump 4 offline indefinitely. The cascade scenario (Pump 3 failure → system collapse) is separated from current conditions by a single mechanical event that is actively materializing. The thesis claims 'sufficient mechanical redundancy' — the system has none. The thesis claims the system can maintain pressure 'during periods of peak demand or localized equipment maintenance' — the system is failing to do so (58 PSI with full reservoir indicates pump-side distribution failure).
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Pump 3 vibration continuing to rise above 40 Hz, Pump 3 temperature exceeding 95°C, any system pressure reading below 50 PSI, any sector hydrant reading below 30 PSI, or — the definitive confirmation — Pump 3 going offline and system pressure dropping below 40 PSI.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: The system is currently in ORDERLY stress — all mandated thresholds are still met (58 PSI > 40 PSI, 51 PSI Sector B > 20 PSI, water quality nominal). However, the stress is orderly only because Pump 3 continues to provide partial output despite active self-destruction. The system's orderly status is dependent on a single component (Pump 3) that is on a mechanically deterministic path toward failure. The transition from orderly to disorderly requires only ONE event: Pump 3 going offline. At that point, the stress becomes self-reinforcing (two pumps at ~150% load will begin degrading rapidly) with no recovery mechanism available (no redundancy, no parts, no demand reduction deployed). The transition from orderly to disorderly will be ABRUPT, not gradual.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: Pump 3 failure is the catalyst. Pump 3 is currently at 38.7 Hz vibration (cavitation active), 102.4% load (overloaded), and 92.5°C (thermal warning triggered). The catalyst is not a hypothetical external shock — it is an internal mechanical process already underway. Proximity: hours to days, not weeks. The specific trigger will be either a thermal auto-trip, cavitation-induced impeller destruction, or bearing seizure.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: The system is heavily pre-loaded on multiple legs simultaneously: (1) Zero redundancy (Pump 4 offline) eliminates all buffer capacity. (2) All active pumps at or above 99.5% load — no headroom for load absorption. (3) One pump (Pump 3) in active self-destruction. (4) High ambient temperature (94°F) compresses thermal margins. (5) Regulatory oversight blind to equipment-level distress. (6) Operator (MWA) showing no emergency escalation. Every leg that could provide resilience is already loaded. The system has consumed all of its safety margins simultaneously.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The thesis claims the system possesses 'robust structural integrity and sufficient mechanical redundancy.' The paradox is that the system is currently meeting its mandated performance thresholds (pressure above minimums, water quality nominal) PRECISELY BECAUSE it is consuming its remaining equipment at an unsustainable rate. The apparent stability is being purchased by irreversible degradation of the asset base. The moment the asset degradation produces a failure (Pump 3 trip), the system does not degrade gradually — it collapses through the cascade mechanism because all buffers have already been spent. The thesis appears partially valid on current snapshot metrics while being fundamentally falsified on the structural conditions that would allow those metrics to be sustained.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals a system in which every actor's rational self-interest reinforces inaction. MWA avoids declaring an emergency because it would trigger costs and scrutiny; the regulator doesn't intervene because MWA's selective reporting shows compliance; fire dispatch has no mechanism for proactive detection. The result is a mechanically deteriorating system surrounded by institutional silence. The data alone shows Pump 3 in active cavitation — but the behavioral analysis reveals that no actor in the system has the incentive, capability, or information to intervene before the cascade trigger. Layer 4 should focus on the gap between the system's current apparent functionality (58 PSI, mandates met) and the structural reality that this functionality depends entirely on a pump that is actively self-destructing, with no recovery mechanism visible, no redundancy available, and no actor moving to change the trajectory.

knowledge_audit.signal: Three-pump aggregate load approaching redundancy kill-switch
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified against thesis falsification criterion #1: Redundant Load Threshold — aggregate load exceeds 115% of rated capacity for >48 hours triggers CRITICAL. Current aggregate: ~301.7% across 3 pumps. Rated 3-pump capacity is 300% (3×100%). This is 100.6% of 3-pump rated capacity, NOT 115% of total system rated capacity. Need to clarify: is the 115% threshold measured against the ACTIVE pump count's rated capacity or against the FULL system's rated capacity (4 pumps = 400%)? If against full system: 301.7/400 = 75.4% — not breached. If against active pumps: 301.7/300 = 100.6% — not at 115% yet. The kill-switch says 'aggregate load on active pumps exceeds 115% of rated capacity' — this is ambiguous. 115% of rated capacity of what denominator?
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: The kill-switch denominator is ambiguous — 115% of rated capacity could mean 115% per pump (already breached by Pump 3 at 102.4% but not by Pumps 1-2), or 115% of the active fleet's total rated capacity (300% for 3 pumps, threshold would be 345%), or 115% of the designed system capacity (400% for 4 pumps, threshold would be 460%). The Layer 1 interpretation assumes the threshold is close to being breached, but the math depends on the denominator.
knowledge_audit.pre_audit_assessment: Severity 9 — assumed kill-switch was nearly breached
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Severity 8 — the zero-redundancy condition is undeniable and critical regardless of the exact 115% calculation. The real danger is structural: any single pump loss means the remaining two must carry ~150% each, which unambiguously exceeds any reasonable interpretation of the 115% threshold.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Reduced from 9 to 8 because the kill-switch is not clearly triggered yet on current data. However, the proximity to trigger upon any single pump failure is extremely high, and the zero-redundancy state is itself a critical structural concern.
